description: The image presents a transparent wireframe 3D model of an automotive body-in-white (BIW) structure, with the front longitudinal beams or crash absorption zones highlighted in solid blue. This schematic illustrates the targeted application locations for low-density Fe-Al-Mn-C steels in lightweight automotive engineering. The blue-highlighted components represent critical energy-absorbing structural elements—specifically the front side members (FSM)—where low-density steel grades can substitute conventional dual-phase steel (DP600) to achieve substantial weight reductions (21–33% according to the paper's LS-DYNA crash simulations) while maintaining crashworthiness under dynamic compression loads (EuroNCAP 40% offset frontal crash protocol). The visualization contextualizes the two lightweight steel families evaluated in the review: the austenite-ferrite alloy (7.4Al-8.2Mn-0.2C) and the fully austenitic grade (9.3Al-25.8Mn-0.96C). The figure supports the central thesis that high-aluminum low-density steels provide a viable metallurgical solution for third-generation automotive lightweighting applications, demonstrating where these materials can be structurally deployed to reduce vehicle mass without compromising mechanical performance or safety-critical energy absorption characteristics.
